seo.title: Supabase vs Firebase vs Appwrite for AI Web Apps
seo.description: Compare three backend platforms by database model, auth, functions, local development, self-hosting, vector workloads, and AI app fit.

content.intro: A backend-as-a-service can turn an AI prototype into a real product by supplying authentication, data storage, file uploads, realtime updates, and server-side functions. Supabase, Firebase, and Appwrite cover that surface with different database models and ownership tradeoffs. The best choice depends less on generated SDK snippets and more on the data relationships, authorization model, deployment constraints, and exit path your application needs.

options.name: Supabase
options.best_for: AI SaaS products that benefit from SQL, relational data, row-level security, and Postgres extensions.

options.name: Firebase
options.best_for: Teams prioritizing mature client SDKs, realtime mobile/web experiences, and deep Google Cloud integration.

options.name: Appwrite
options.tagline: Open-source backend platform with cloud and self-hosted options
options.best_for: Developers wanting a cohesive API platform and a documented path to running the backend themselves.

criteria.name: Primary database model
criteria.description: The data model developers design around.

values.Supabase: PostgreSQL relational database with SQL and extensions
values.Firebase: Cloud Firestore document database plus Realtime Database
values.Appwrite: Document-oriented databases exposed through Appwrite APIs

criteria.name: Relational workloads
criteria.description: Fit for joins, constraints, reporting, and transactional SaaS data.

values.Supabase: Native Postgres relationships, constraints, views, and SQL
values.Firebase: Denormalization is common; joins live in application logic
values.Appwrite: Relationships are supported through its database APIs, without full SQL semantics

criteria.name: Authorization
criteria.description: How access rules are enforced near stored data.

values.Supabase: JWT-based Auth integrates with PostgreSQL row-level security
values.Firebase: Security Rules protect Firestore, Storage, and Realtime Database
values.Appwrite: Document, file, and resource permissions integrate with Auth teams and users

criteria.name: Server-side functions
criteria.description: Where secret-bearing AI API calls can run.

values.Supabase: Edge Functions with documented auth patterns
values.Firebase: Cloud Functions with HTTP and event triggers
values.Appwrite: Containerized Functions triggered by HTTP, events, schedules, or SDK calls

criteria.name: Vector and retrieval workloads
criteria.description: Suitability for embedding storage and filtered semantic search.

values.Supabase: Postgres extensions such as pgvector keep vectors near relational metadata
values.Firebase: Vector search capabilities exist in Firestore, within its document model and query constraints
values.Appwrite: Typically pairs with a dedicated vector service or custom function for advanced retrieval

criteria.name: Local development
criteria.description: Ability to test auth, data, and functions without touching production.

values.Firebase: Local Emulator Suite covers major Firebase services
values.Appwrite: Self-hosted installation and local function workflows support isolated development

criteria.name: Self-hosting
criteria.description: Whether the core platform can run under your operational control.

values.Supabase: Open-source self-hosting is documented, with operational responsibility on the team
values.Firebase: Managed Google service; emulators are for development, not a production self-hosting path
values.Appwrite: Open-source self-hosting is a first-class deployment option

criteria.name: Realtime data
criteria.description: Built-in support for live client updates.

values.Supabase: Realtime changes and channels integrate with Postgres
values.Firebase: Realtime listeners are a foundational client SDK capability
values.Appwrite: Realtime subscriptions cover database, auth, storage, and function events

criteria.name: Migration posture
criteria.description: How to keep an eventual backend change manageable.

values.Supabase: Standard Postgres tools reduce database-level lock-in, though platform APIs still need migration
values.Firebase: Document schemas, Security Rules, and service integrations can make migration substantial
values.Appwrite: Open source improves deployment choice, but applications remain coupled to Appwrite APIs

verdict.summary: Supabase is the default when an AI webapp needs relational product data and vector search in one Postgres system. Firebase remains excellent for client-heavy realtime products and teams already committed to Google services. Appwrite is attractive when a coherent backend API and self-hosting option matter more than SQL. In every case, keep model keys in server-side functions and test authorization rules independently of the generated UI.

use_cases.scenario: Multi-tenant AI SaaS with billing and RAG
use_cases.reason: Relational constraints, row-level security, and Postgres vector extensions fit linked tenant, document, and subscription data.
Its mature client SDKs, listeners, auth, and emulator workflow support reactive mobile and web clients.
It packages auth, databases, storage, realtime, and functions behind a consistent API with a self-hosted path.
